text: Seneca's letters to Lucilius are the most intimate record we have of Stoic practice under pressure, and assent appears in them as a daily skill rather than a philosophical thesis. Seneca describes sitting with a disagreeable impression — say, the perception that a friend has slighted him — and deliberately not completing the inference that anger demands. He does not deny the impression; he suspends its consequence. Scholars such as Brad Inwood have noted that Seneca transforms Chrysippean logic into something closer to what we might call cognitive restructuring: identify the automatic interpretation, question whether it is the only one, choose a reading consistent with virtue. Theory becomes technique.
question: What technique does Brad Inwood suggest Seneca derived from Chrysippean logic?

options.text: Memorizing philosophical maxims to recite when passions arise
Avoiding situations likely to produce disagreeable impressions
Cognitive restructuring: questioning automatic interpretations and choosing virtuous readings
Focusing attention on physical sensations rather than mental narratives
